sanskrit_devanagari:
यज्ञार्थात्कर्मणोऽन्यत्र लोकोऽयं कर्मबन्धनः ।
तदर्थं कर्म कौन्तेय मुक्तसङ्गः समाचर ॥ ९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yajñārthāt karmaṇo 'nyatra loko 'yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ |
tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara || 9 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Work done as a sacrifice for Viṣṇu has to be performed; otherwise work causes bondage in this material world. Therefore, O son of Kuntī, perform your prescribed duties for His satisfaction, and in that way you will always remain free from bondage.

Work done as a sacrifice for Vishnu has to be performed, otherwise work causes bondage in this material world. Therefore, O son of Kunti, perform your prescribed duties for His satisfaction, and in that way you will always remain free from bondage.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1660 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since one has to work even for the simple maintenance of the body, the prescribed duties for a particular social position and quality are so made that that purpose can be fulfilled. Yajña means Lord Viṣṇu, or sacrificial performances.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.9 Ayam, this; lokah, man, the one who is eligible for action; karma-bandhanah, becomes bound by actions- the person who has karma as his bondage (bandhana) is karma-bandhanah-; anyatra, other than; that karmanah, action; yajnarthat, meant for Got not by that meant for God.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-9-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: yajña = Viṣṇu (citing Tai. Sam. 1.7.4: 'Sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu'). Action FOR yajña = action for God. Action other than this binds the world (loko 'yam karma-bandhanaḥ); action for yajña, performed mukta-saṅgaḥ (free from attachment), does not bind. The verse establishes the FRAME: orientation toward yajña (= toward God) is what distinguishes binding from non-binding action. The action itself is identical; the orientation differs.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.9 The world is imprisoned by the bond of work only when work is done for personal ends, but not when work is performed or money acquired for the purpose of sacrifice etc. prescribed in the scriptures. So, for the purpose of sacrifice, you must perform acts like the acquisition of money.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-9-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: yajñārthāt karmaṇaḥ broadly: action for the purpose of sacrifice prescribed in the scriptures, AND any acquisition or work performed in the spirit of yajña. The world is imprisoned by the bond of work only when work is done for personal ends; for sacrifice, no bondage. mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara — perform the work, having overcome attachments generated by personal ambition. The verse establishes the frame; everything in 3.10-3.16 elaborates the cosmology that supports the frame.

positions.rendering: as a yajna to the Supreme Lord
for the sake of yajña, where yajña = Viṣṇu (per Tai. Sam. 1.7.4: 'Sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu')
for the purpose of sacrifice prescribed in the scriptures; broader: any acquisition or work done in the spirit of yajña

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: yajñārthāt is the verse's frame-pivot. The Advaita reading takes yajña = Viṣṇu (the deity behind sacrifice); the action is for God. The Vishishtadvaita reading takes yajña as both the literal sacrifices and the broader scope of any work performed as worship. Prabhupada inherits the Advaita gloss explicitly. Mukundananda preserves the theistic frame. The doctrinally important point all four agree on: the FRAME of action's purpose distinguishes binding from non-binding action. Engineering layers must preserve this — the action itself is not the pivot; the orientation toward yajña is.

literal_meaning: This world is bound by action other than action performed for yajña (sacrifice); therefore, son of Kuntī, perform action for that purpose, free from attachment.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.9 is the yajña pivot. yajñārthāt karmaṇo 'nyatra loko 'yaṁ karma-bandhanaḥ — this world (or this person) is bound by action other than that performed for the sake of yajña. tad-arthaṁ karma kaunteya mukta-saṅgaḥ samācara — therefore perform action for that purpose, free from attachment. Per the Advaita reading: yajña = Viṣṇu (per the Vedic 'sacrifice is verily Viṣṇu'); whatever is done for him is yajña-artham. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: yajña includes the prescribed sacrifices and the broader complex of duty-performed-as-worship; the world is imprisoned by personal-end action and freed by yajña-oriented action. The verse establishes the FRAME that distinguishes binding from non-binding action.
